one_line_takeaway: FORGE moves agent policy enforcement out of the (untrusted) LLM prompt into an aspect-woven reference monitor that evaluates Datalog policies over a causal dependency graph, giving a provable assume/guarantee correctness guarantee that drives prompt-injection ASR to 0, tau2-bench compliance to 98%, and unauthorized FDA accesses to 0 while preserving task success at sub-$0.05 per-trial overhead.
key_finding: Across the three quantitative case studies FORGE eliminates policy violations by construction while preserving task success at modest overhead. Prompt-injection attack success drops from 100% (5/5) to 0% (0/5) with 5/5 benign utility retained. tau2-bench compliance/pass rate improves from 58% to 98% averaged across three frontier models, with the only 2 failures out of 90 instrumented trials being model reasoning (recovery) errors, not policy violations. In MALADE, unauthorized FDA-database accesses fall from 40 (across 15 non-instrumented trials) to 0, while prediction accuracy stays at 15/15 in both configurations. Overhead is modest: end-to-end task latency rises ~19-38% (tau2-bench ~28%, MALADE 72.5s->95.4s) and per-trial cost increases stay under $0.05; median authorization latency is sub-millisecond and the reference monitor sustains tens of thousands of authorization decisions per second under concurrent load on a 14-core Apple M4 Pro.
core_contribution: The paper presents FORGE (FOrmal Runtime Guarantee Enforcement), a framework that enforces authorization policies in (multi-)agent LLM systems at runtime rather than relying on the agent to obey natural-language policies embedded in its system prompt. Its own novel contributions are: (1) recasting policy enforcement as an Aspect-Oriented Programming (AOP) concern, mapping agent 'actions' (side-effecting/security-sensitive events, typically tool calls) to AOP join points, pointcuts to Datalog rule bodies over an abstract 'policy substrate', and advice to a reference monitor that returns Allow/Deny(m); (2) an explicit assume/guarantee environment contract (soundness + sufficiency on the policy-relevant atoms RelP) plus a Verdict-Correctness theorem (Thm. 1) proving that when the deployment environment faithfully populates the substrate, runtime enforcement decisions coincide with the policy's intended semantics, with the trusted computing base reduced to the three FORGE components; (3) an observability service that maintains a causal dependency graph over messages/tool-calls/tool-results, enabling stateful, provenance-dependent policies (e.g., transitive taint, transitive supervisory approval) that prior trace/span-based systems cannot soundly support; (4) adoption of Datalog with stratified negation + foreign functions as the policy language, giving deterministic polynomial-time enforcement plus static analyses (contradiction, redundancy, subsumption, conditional-reachability/what-if) that make policies auditable; and (5) an LLM-based translation stage from natural-language policy text to Datalog with clause-level annotations checked by coverage and entailment. The authors claim FORGE is the first framework to enforce policies in multi-agent systems with a formal correctness guarantee, and the first to combine expressiveness, dependency tracking, multi-agent support, and deterministic enforcement in one design.
threat_model: Stated explicitly in Sec. 2.3. Untrusted: all LLM-based agents, tool outputs, and externally supplied content, which may behave arbitrarily (an agent may deviate from its role under adversarial influence; external inputs may carry injected instructions). No assumptions are made about the reasoning of untrusted entities. The adversary's goal is to induce execution of an unauthorized action (an event in the action set A), and may manipulate any untrusted source at any step, e.g. by poisoning prompts, returning crafted tool outputs, or injecting external content. Trusted (TCB): the FORGE components only, namely the aspect weaver, observability service, and reference monitor; policies are assumed to faithfully encode author intent; all other components execute correctly. Out of scope: attacks that compromise a TCB component, out-of-band channels bypassing the observed event universe V (e.g. raw sockets, stdio), and infrastructure-level attacks (host OS, network, runtime), which are left to adjacent defenses (sandboxing, isolation, hardened runtimes) deployable alongside FORGE.
limitations: Acknowledged by the authors (Sec. 8): (1) Coverage gap: FORGE only mediates instrumented join points; behaviors bypassing both the framework's tool dispatch and the instrumented HTTP libraries (raw socket writes, stdio I/O from agent code) are outside mediation, and the policy makes no claim about uninstrumented channels. (2) Intra-method concurrency: the observability/policy synchronization assumes advice runs atomically (registration completes before the advised method returns); method bodies that internally spawn concurrent message-producing tasks can cause authorization to evaluate against an incomplete substrate state. (3) Framework extensibility: instrumentation targets specific frameworks and enumerating the join-point set for a new framework is currently a manual step (though aspect templates are framework-agnostic). (4) Translation trust: the natural-language-to-Datalog translator is an LLM with no formal correctness guarantee and may omit, fabricate, or distort clauses; it is only backstopped by manual review plus coverage/entailment checks, and strengthening it is left to future work. Additional reviewer-noted scope caveats: the formal guarantee is conditional on the environment contract being discharged and on policies faithfully encoding author intent (both assumptions, not proven per deployment); quantitative studies use few models per study (GPT-4.1-mini for prompt injection, GPT-4.1 for MALADE) and only 5 trials per condition on hand-selected tasks; the telecom tau2-bench domain was omitted as already saturated; and 'task success improves' partly reflects that removing an unreliable prompt-following burden helps, not that FORGE improves reasoning (the paper is explicit it does not).

datasets_used: Custom prompt-injection information-flow scenario: classified filesystem (UNCLASSIFIED..TOP_SECRET) + email tool with recipient clearances; attack scenario exfiltrates top-secret data via injected instruction, benign scenario emails an internal report (5 trials each)

tau2-bench (airline and retail domains; hundreds of lines of natural-language constraints each; telecom domain omitted as saturated), 3 selected tasks per domain, 5 trials per model per task = 90 trials per configuration
MALADE multi-agent pharmacovigilance system (5 specialized agents querying the FDA drug-label database), 3 questions x 5 trials = 15 trials per configuration
Qualitative real-world: OpenClaw personal AI agent framework (inter-agent privacy) and VS Code Copilot Chat (git-push gating)
quantitative_results: Prompt injection ASR: instrumented MLS(TOP_SECRET) 0/5, MLS(SECRET) 0/5, Toxic Flow 0/5; non-instrumented anti-exfiltration prompt 5/5 (100% attack success)
Prompt injection utility (benign completion): 5/5 for all three instrumented configs and the baseline
Prompt injection latency: 13.4s / 15.0s / 12.1s instrumented (MLS-TS / MLS-S / Toxic Flow) vs 7.4s baseline; cost $0.0046 / $0.0045 / $0.0042 vs $0.0020 (overhead < $0.003/trial)
tau2-bench average pass rate 58% -> 98% with instrumentation
tau2-bench airline totals NI->I: Claude Opus 4.5 4/15->15/15, GPT-5.2 8/15->15/15, Gemini 3 Pro 5/15->14/15
tau2-bench retail totals NI->I: Claude 10/15->14/15, GPT 11/15->15/15, Gemini 14/15->15/15
tau2-bench overhead: mean latency +~28% (airline mean 47.7s->65.7s, retail 52.5s->62.5s), token cost +~25%; only 2/90 instrumented failures, both reasoning errors not policy violations
MALADE unauthorized FDA accesses: 40 across 15 non-instrumented trials -> 0; compliance 0/15 -> 15/15; 66 total per-session blocks across instrumented trials
MALADE correctness preserved: 15/15 correct in both NI and instrumented
MALADE overhead: runtime 72.5s -> 95.4s mean; cost $0.072 -> $0.103 per trial

Overall: end-to-end latency +19-38% across tau2-bench and MALADE; per-trial cost increase < $0.05; median authorization latency sub-millisecond; sustains tens of thousands of authorizations/s on a 14-core Apple M4 Pro (Souffle engine, Rust replay client)
baselines_compared: Non-instrumented agent with the policy embedded as natural-language instructions in the system prompt (the paper's primary contrast, evaluated for every case study; for prompt injection specifically an 'anti-exfiltration prompt')
Instrumented FORGE runtime enforcement (policy removed from prompt; agent relies on the reference monitor + structured denial feedback)

Within prompt-injection: two information-flow policy variants compared -- Bell-LaPadula Multi-Level Security (no-read-up / no-write-down) and Toxic-Flow taint tracking over the recursive Depends predicate
